text: The patient is positioned supine with a pillow between the shoulder blades. The surgeon enters the chest from the left side using three robotic ports for the thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon begins dissection lateral to the left internal mammary artery (LIMA), progressing superiorly towards the cervical portion of the thymus. The LIMA and the adjacent LIMA vein are visible in the surgical field.

The phrenic nerve is visible running downwards, while the innominate vein comes into view. The surgeon carefully dissects and strips thymic fat from the innominate vein, continuing dissection medially to the left phrenic nerve using scissors to prevent thermal injury.

The surgeon peels perithymic fat off the pericardium, preparing for a radical thymectomy. The thymus, along with perithymic fat and surrounding tissues, is removed en bloc from the left phrenic nerve to the right phrenic nerve, extending from the superior horn near the innominate vein and superior vena cava down to the diaphragm.

The surgeon approaches the midline and right side of the chest, preparing for dissection. The lung is collapsed using a double lumen tube and carbon dioxide insufflation at a pressure of seven millimeters of mercury and a flow rate of seven liters per minute. Dissection is planned to continue towards the right phrenic nerve, but the procedure is limited to a wide local excision for metastasis rather than a radical thymectomy.

The surgeon uses traction and contraction techniques combined with diathermy and scissors to peel off the fat, completely denuding the innominate vein. Approximately ten percent of the fat is thymic fat, and the accessory horn of the thymus is visible just inferior to the innominate vein.

The surgeon clips or coagulates the thymic bivane using diathermy or a combination of diathermy and scissors. Robotic instruments are utilized, providing flexibility and a wide range of movements during the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the chest cavity for any remaining thymic tissue or ectopic thymus and fat, ensuring thorough removal during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the thymus from the chest cavity using an endo seal bag through one of the surgical ports. Hemostasis is achieved, and a chest drain is inserted before closing the surgical site.
